Precalculus for Environmental Science: Foundations for Modeling Gaj Sivandran 2026-07-03 Chapter 1 Why Precalculus? A biologist notices that a lake’s oxygen level swings up and down with the seasons, peaking every summer and bottoming out every winter. A hydrologist watches a contaminant plume spread outward from a spill site in a widening circle. A climate scientist fits a curve to fifty years of temperature data to project what next decade might look like. None of these questions can be answered with arithmetic alone. They require a language for describing shapes of change—lines, circles, curves, cycles—and the tools to translate a real system into an equation you can actually work with. That language is precalculus. Welcome to Precalculus for Environmental Science. This book is your on-ramp to the Environmental Calculus sequence. It is not a re-run of your high school math class. Every tool you build here—coordinate systems, functions, exponentials, trigonometry—is chosen because you will use it, directly, to model a real environmental system in the chapters and courses that follow. 1.1 Why This Book? Most precalculus textbooks are written to prepare students for any future math course, so their examples tend to be abstract: generic curves, unlabeled axes, “find x.” That approach works, but it misses an opportunity—precalculus concepts map beautifully onto environmental systems, and concepts stick best when they’re connected to something you care about. In this book, every major idea is anchored in an environmental context: Lines model steady trends—rising sea level, a linear dose-response relationship. Circles describe buffer zones, contamination radii, and orbital paths. Exponentials and logarithms govern decay, growth, and scales like pH. Trigonometry and sinusoids capture cycles—tides, seasons, daylight hours. Our goal isn’t just to get you ready for the next exam. It’s to help you see precalculus as a toolkit you’ll reach for constantly as an environmental scientist. 1.2 What Is Precalculus, Really? At its core, precalculus is the study of relationships between quantities—and the vocabulary for describing how those relationships behave. Here are a few examples you’ll encounter in this book: System The Relationship The Precalculus Tool Atmosphere CO₂ concentration rising roughly steadily over decades Linear functions Toxicology A drug or pollutant concentration decaying over time Exponential functions Water chemistry Acidity spanning many orders of magnitude Logarithmic scales Tides & seasons A quantity that rises and falls in a repeating cycle Sinusoidal functions Contamination The boundary of an affected area around a point source Circles & coordinate geometry Notice the pattern: each real system has a shape, and each shape has a matching mathematical family. Learning to recognize which family fits a given system—and then finding the specific equation—is the central skill of this course. Shapes of Change Think of an environmental quantity you’ve read about recently (temperature, a pollutant level, a population count, tide height). In a small group, discuss: does it change steadily, does it grow or shrink multiplicatively, or does it repeat in cycles? What shape would its graph have? Concept Check: Matching Systems to Math See if you can match each scenario to the type of function that best describes it. 1. A tank drains so that the volume of water decreases by the same number of liters every minute. Linear, exponential, or sinusoidal? Click to reveal answer Linear. A constant rate of change (same amount lost every minute) is the hallmark of a linear relationship. 2. A radioactive tracer’s activity drops by half every 8 days. Linear, exponential, or sinusoidal? Click to reveal answer Exponential. A fixed fraction lost per time interval (a “half-life”) is the signature of exponential decay. 3. The number of daylight hours in Seattle rises and falls smoothly over the course of a year, repeating annually. Linear, exponential, or sinusoidal? Click to reveal answer Sinusoidal. A repeating, cyclical pattern is best captured by a sine or cosine function. 1.3 How This Book Works Each chapter follows the same supportive structure: Explanations with environmental context. New ideas are introduced in plain language first, then formalized, always tied to a real environmental system. Prompt boxes are places to pause and think—some ask you to discuss with a partner, some ask you to sketch or write, some pose a question to sit with before reading on: Example Prompt This is where you’ll find a question or activity. The write-in space below is for your notes. Concept checks appear throughout each chapter—low-stakes, click-to-reveal questions so you can immediately test whether an idea has clicked. Worked examples walk through environmental problems step by step: what we’re doing, why, and what the answer means in context. Skills drills at the end of each chapter give you pure mechanical repetition—no context, just reps—to build fluency with the algebra itself. Workbook chapters (added in a later phase of this book) will follow each content chapter, giving you guided practice, write-in prompts, and additional problems. 1.4 Success in This Class First and foremost, know this: we believe in your ability to learn and grow. You bring unique strengths and perspectives, and your effort matters here. If precalculus material feels rusty or unfamiliar, that’s completely normal—it doesn’t mean you don’t belong in environmental science. Mistakes are how you find out where your thinking needs refinement, and that’s exactly where growth happens. Just like fieldwork, learning math is iterative: try, test, revise, improve. Give yourself permission to be imperfect while you build these tools—you’ll need them, and you’re capable of mastering them. 1.5 Looking Ahead By the end of this book, you will be able to: Track units and coordinates through environmental calculations and check results for reasonableness Build and interpret linear, quadratic, exponential, logarithmic, rational, and sinusoidal models from real environmental data Compose and invert functions to move between related environmental quantities Use trigonometry and circular motion to describe cyclical environmental processes Read, graph, and transform functions to communicate environmental trends with mathematical precision These are exactly the tools you’ll need on day one of Environmental Calculus. 1.6 About This Book This book is a companion volume to Environmental Calculus, written to prepare students for that course using the same environmental-science lens. It is a work in progress—expect rough edges, and expect them to get smoothed out with your help. If you spot an error, or a section that doesn’t quite click, please let us know so we can improve it for you and for future students.

Chapter 2 Foundations: Numbers, Units & Coordinate Systems 2.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Convert between units using dimensional analysis, including compound and derived units. Plot and interpret points in a coordinate system. Compute the distance between two points and interpret it in context. Build simple models using coordinates and units drawn from environmental monitoring data. 2.2 Introduction Imagine you’ve just joined a team monitoring water quality in a small watershed. You’re handed a spreadsheet: a list of sampling stations, each with a location, a nitrate concentration, and a date. Before you can plot a single trend line or fit a single model, you need to answer some very basic-sounding questions: Are these concentrations in the same units as the regulatory limit you’re comparing them to? How far apart are these two sampling stations, actually? If I place these stations on a map, where do they sit relative to each other? None of these questions require calculus. They require the tools in this chapter: units, coordinates, and distance. They are the quiet infrastructure underneath every model you’ll build in this course — get them wrong, and everything built on top of them is wrong too. Before We Start Think of a time a mismatched unit caused confusion — a recipe, a road sign, a medication dose, a shipping label. What went wrong, and how was it caught (or not)? 2.3 Unit Conversion 2.3.1 Dimensional Analysis: Multiplying by 1 The safest way to convert between units is dimensional analysis: multiply by a fraction that equals 1, where the numerator and denominator are the same quantity expressed in different units. Because you’re multiplying by 1, the value of the quantity never changes — only its units do. For example, since \(1000 \text{ m} = 1 \text{ km}\), the fraction \[\frac{1 \text{ km}}{1000 \text{ m}} = 1\] You can multiply any measurement by this fraction (or its flip) without changing what it represents — only how it’s expressed. Worked Example: Converting a Flow Rate A stream gauge reports a flow rate of \(2.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}\). What is this in liters per minute? Step 1 — List your conversion facts. \(1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}\) \(1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}\) Step 2 — Chain the conversion factors so unwanted units cancel. \[ 2.4\ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} \times \frac{1000\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{m}^3} \times \frac{60\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{min}} = 2.4 \times 1000 \times 60 \ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}} = 144{,}000 \ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}} \] Step 3 — Check reasonableness. A flow of a few cubic meters per second is a real, moderately-sized stream, so a six-figure liters-per-minute value is plausible — it’s just a much smaller time unit and much smaller volume unit multiplied together. 2.3.2 A Subtlety: Concentration Units Environmental data often reports concentration in mg/L (milligrams per liter) or in ppm (parts per million). For dilute aqueous solutions — which describes most natural water — 1 liter of water has a mass of very close to 1000 grams (1 kg), so: \[ 1\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \approx 1\ \text{ppm} \quad \text{(dilute water only)} \] This is a convenient approximation, not a universal law — it relies on the density of the solution being close to that of pure water, which breaks down for very concentrated solutions, for other fluids like air or soil, or when working by mass fraction in a non-aqueous medium. Always double check which convention a dataset or regulation is using before assuming the shortcut applies. 2.3.3 Worked Example: An Emissions Rate Conversion A facility reports emitting 18 metric tons of CO₂ per year. Regulators want this expressed in kilograms per day. Step 1 — List the givens and the target. Given: \(18\) metric tons / year Target: kg / day Step 2 — List conversion facts. \(1\) metric ton \(= 1000\) kg \(1\) year \(\approx 365\) days Step 3 — Chain the factors. \[ 18\ \frac{\text{tons}}{\text{yr}} \times \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{1\ \text{ton}} \times \frac{1\ \text{yr}}{365\ \text{days}} \approx 49.3\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} \] Step 4 — Interpret. This facility emits roughly 49 kg of CO₂ every day — a way of expressing the same yearly total that’s easier to compare against a daily operational limit. 2.3.4 A Famous Example: The Mars Climate Orbiter  Mismatched units aren’t just a classroom nuisance — they’ve caused real, expensive failures. In 1999, NASA’s Mars Climate Orbiter was lost as it approached Mars. The investigation found that one engineering team had provided thrust data in pound-seconds (an imperial unit), while the navigation software on the other end of the pipeline expected the value in newton-seconds (the metric unit). The software used the number as given, without converting it, and the resulting navigation error sent the spacecraft catastrophically off course. No individual calculation was “wrong” — every team did their own arithmetic correctly. The failure was a unit mismatch at the handoff between two systems: a number changed hands without its units being checked. It’s the same failure mode as forgetting to convert mg/L to ppm before comparing a reading to a regulatory limit — just with a much higher price tag. The lesson scales down perfectly to a spreadsheet: always track units through every step, and always double-check them at every handoff, not just within your own calculation. Concept Check: Unit Conversion 1. A monitoring station reports a pollutant level of 3 mg/L in a dilute water sample. Approximately how many ppm is this? Click to reveal answer About 3 ppm — for dilute aqueous solutions, mg/L and ppm are approximately interchangeable. 2. You need to convert 5 acres to square meters, and you know 1 acre ≈ 4047 m². Which fraction would you multiply by? Click to reveal answer \[5\ \text{acres} \times \frac{4047\ \text{m}^2}{1\ \text{acre}}\] The acre units are in the denominator of the conversion factor so they cancel with the acres you’re converting from, leaving m². 3. Why doesn’t the mg/L ≈ ppm shortcut work for a very salty brine solution? Click to reveal answer The shortcut relies on 1 liter of solution having a mass close to 1000 g (the density of pure water). A concentrated brine is noticeably denser than pure water, so the same mass of solute represents a different ppm value than it would in dilute water. 2.4 Coordinate Systems 2.4.1 Locating Points in a Plane A coordinate system lets us assign a precise location to a point using numbers. In the Cartesian plane, every point is described by an ordered pair \((x, y)\): how far to move horizontally (\(x\)) and vertically (\(y\)) from a fixed reference point called the origin, \((0,0)\). For environmental work, the origin and axes are often chosen to make the numbers convenient — for instance, “meters east” and “meters north” of a reference stake at a field site, rather than raw latitude and longitude (we’ll work with true geographic coordinates once we cover angular measure in Chapter 7). Setting Up a Local Grid A field team sets up a reference stake at a wetland restoration site and calls it \((0, 0)\). They record each new sampling point as meters east (x) and meters north (y) of that stake. Station A is 40 m east and 30 m north of the stake. Station B is 10 m west and 60 m north. What are the coordinates of each, using the convention that west and south are negative? 2.4.2 Environmental Application: A Monitoring Network The table and plot below show five sampling stations placed around a small watershed, using a local meters-east / meters-north coordinate grid centered on the stream gauge. Table 2.1: Sampling station locations relative to the stream gauge. Station East (m) North (m) Gauge (origin) 0 0 Upstream Well -120 80 Downstream Well 180 -40 Wetland Inlet 60 150 Wetland Outlet 95 210 Figure 2.1: Sampling stations plotted on a local coordinate grid centered on the stream gauge. Notice that a negative x-coordinate means “west of the gauge,” and a negative y-coordinate means “south of the gauge” — exactly like the four quadrants of a standard Cartesian plane. 2.5 The Distance Formula 2.5.1 Deriving the Distance Formula How far apart are two stations, say the Upstream Well at \((-120, 80)\) and the Downstream Well at \((180, -40)\)? The horizontal separation and vertical separation form two legs of a right triangle, and the straight-line distance between the points is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean theorem: \[ d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} \] This is the distance formula — it’s simply the Pythagorean theorem written in coordinate form. 2.5.2 Worked Example: Distance Between Two Wells Using the Upstream Well \((-120, 80)\) and Downstream Well \((180, -40)\): Step 1 — Identify the coordinates. \((x_1, y_1) = (-120, 80)\), \((x_2, y_2) = (180, -40)\) Step 2 — Substitute into the formula. \[ d = \sqrt{(180 - (-120))^2 + (-40 - 80)^2} = \sqrt{(300)^2 + (-120)^2} \] Step 3 — Simplify. \[ d = \sqrt{90000 + 14400} = \sqrt{104400} \approx 323.1 \text{ m} \] Step 4 — Interpret. The two wells are roughly 323 meters apart in a straight line — useful information if, for example, you’re deciding whether contamination could realistically spread from one well to the other through groundwater flow in a given time. Concept Check: Distance Formula 1. Two air quality sensors sit at \((0, 0)\) and \((6, 8)\), in kilometers. How far apart are they? Click to reveal answer \[d = \sqrt{(6-0)^2 + (8-0)^2} = \sqrt{36+64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ km}\] 2. If two points have the same y-coordinate, what does the distance formula simplify to? Click to reveal answer The \((y_2-y_1)^2\) term becomes 0, so \(d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2} = |x_2 - x_1|\) — just the horizontal separation. This matches intuition: if two points sit on the same horizontal line, the distance between them is simply how far apart they are left-to-right. 3. Why does the distance formula use squared differences rather than just \((x_2-x_1) + (y_2-y_1)\)? Click to reveal answer Because the formula comes from the Pythagorean theorem, which relates the squared side lengths of a right triangle. Squaring also ensures a negative difference doesn’t cancel out a positive one — distance should always come out non-negative. 2.6 Practice: Multi-Step Conversions 2.6.1 What Are You Practicing? These problems combine unit conversion with basic interpretation, using data from the watershed monitoring network introduced above. Some problems require you to convert units before you can compare or combine numbers; others ask you to combine a unit conversion with a coordinate or distance calculation. 2.6.2 How to Approach These Problems Identify what units you’re given and what units you need. Write out the conversion factor(s) you need as fractions equal to 1. Chain the factors so unwanted units cancel. Once units are consistent, do the underlying arithmetic or geometry (a distance calculation, a comparison, etc.). Check whether your final answer is a reasonable size for the real-world quantity it represents. 2.6.3 Practice Problems 1. The wetland inlet station reports a nitrate concentration of 0.008 g/L. Express this in mg/L, then decide whether it exceeds a 5 ppm regulatory threshold (for this dilute sample, treat mg/L ≈ ppm). Click to reveal solution \(0.008\ \text{g/L} \times \dfrac{1000\ \text{mg}}{1\ \text{g}} = 8\ \text{mg/L} \approx 8\ \text{ppm}\), which exceeds the 5 ppm threshold. 2. A well pump moves water at 15 gallons per minute. Given \(1\) gallon \(\approx 3.785\) L, express this rate in liters per hour. Click to reveal solution \[ 15\ \frac{\text{gal}}{\text{min}} \times \frac{3.785\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{gal}} \times \frac{60\ \text{min}}{1\ \text{hr}} \approx 3406.5\ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{hr}} \] 3. Using the monitoring station table above, find the straight-line distance between the Wetland Inlet \((60, 150)\) and Wetland Outlet \((95, 210)\), in meters. Click to reveal solution \[ d = \sqrt{(95-60)^2 + (210-150)^2} = \sqrt{35^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{1225 + 3600} = \sqrt{4825} \approx 69.5 \text{ m} \] 4. A sensor logs temperature in degrees Fahrenheit, but your model needs Celsius. Using \(C = \dfrac{5}{9}(F - 32)\), convert a reading of 68°F to Celsius, then explain in one sentence why this conversion is not a simple multiply-by-a-fraction dimensional analysis problem like the others in this section. Click to reveal solution \(C = \frac{5}{9}(68-32) = \frac{5}{9}(36) = 20\)°C. Unlike the other conversions, this one requires subtracting 32 first — the Fahrenheit and Celsius scales don’t share a common zero point, so the conversion is an affine function (multiply and shift), not a pure scaling. 5. A watershed’s annual sediment load is reported as 2,400,000 kg/year. Express this as metric tons per day (1 metric ton = 1000 kg; use 365 days/year). Click to reveal solution \[ 2{,}400{,}000\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{yr}} \times \frac{1\ \text{ton}}{1000\ \text{kg}} \times \frac{1\ \text{yr}}{365\ \text{days}} \approx 6.58\ \frac{\text{tons}}{\text{day}} \] 6. The Upstream Well sits at \((-120, 80)\). A new proposed well site is 50 m directly east and 50 m directly south of it. What are the coordinates of the new site, and how far is it from the stream gauge at the origin? Click to reveal solution New site: \((-120+50,\ 80-50) = (-70, 30)\). Distance from origin: \(d = \sqrt{(-70)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{4900+900} = \sqrt{5800} \approx 76.2\) m. 2.7 Skills Drills These drills strip away the environmental context so you can build raw fluency with the mechanics. Show your work, then check your answer. 2.7.1 Unit Conversion Chains Convert each quantity to the target unit. \(3.2\) km to m \(750\) mL to L \(4.5\) hours to seconds \(2\) metric tons to kg \(60\) mph to m/s (use \(1\) mile \(\approx 1609\) m) \(120\) mg/L to g/L Click to reveal answers \(3200\) m \(0.75\) L \(16{,}200\) s \(2000\) kg \(\approx 26.8\) m/s \(0.12\) g/L 2.7.2 Plotting Points Plot each point on a coordinate plane and state which quadrant (or axis) it falls on. \((4, 5)\) \((-3, 2)\) \((-6, -1)\) \((7, -2)\) \((0, -4)\) \((-5, 0)\) Click to reveal answers Quadrant I Quadrant II Quadrant III Quadrant IV On the y-axis On the x-axis 2.7.3 Distance Calculations Find the distance between each pair of points. \((0,0)\) and \((3,4)\) \((1,1)\) and \((4,5)\) \((-2,3)\) and \((2,-3)\) \((5,0)\) and \((0,12)\) \((-1,-1)\) and \((-4,-5)\) Click to reveal answers \(5\) \(5\) \(\sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21\) \(13\) \(5\) Test 1 Preview This chapter is the first of three chapters covered on Test 1. Chapter 4 Functions and Graphs 4.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Determine whether a relationship is a function from a graph, table, or equation. Identify the domain and range of a function in context. Read and interpret transformations of a graph. Build and evaluate piecewise (multipart) functions. 4.2 Function Definition & Notation A function is a rule that assigns exactly one output to each input. If \(x\) is the input and \(y\) is the output, we write \(y = f(x)\), read “\(f\) of \(x\).” The name \(f\) is arbitrary — you’ll often see functions named for what they represent, like \(C(t)\) for concentration as a function of time, or \(T(h)\) for temperature as a function of elevation. The defining rule — exactly one output per input — is what separates a function from a general relationship. If you can trace a vertical line through a graph and it crosses the curve more than once, the graph does not represent a function of \(x\), because that one input \(x\) would correspond to more than one output. Is It a Function? A graph shows water depth in a tidal channel over the course of a day — depth on the vertical axis, time of day on the horizontal axis. Could two different times of day have the exact same depth? Does that make it not a function? Discuss with a partner why or why not. Concept Check: Function vs. Non-Function 1. A table lists 5 different sampling dates and the nitrate concentration measured on each. Is concentration a function of date? Click to reveal answer Yes — each date has exactly one recorded concentration. (Two different dates could, in principle, have the same concentration value — that’s fine. What would break the function rule is one date having two different recorded concentrations.) 2. A graph plots elevation (vertical axis) against distance along a river channel that loops back on itself, so a single distance-along-the-channel value corresponds to two different elevations where the loop crosses itself. Is elevation a function of distance here? Click to reveal answer No. One input (a given distance along the channel) maps to two different outputs (two different elevations), which violates the function rule. 4.3 Domain & Range The domain of a function is the set of all valid inputs; the range is the set of all possible outputs. In environmental modeling, domain and range are rarely “all real numbers” — they’re restricted by what’s physically or practically meaningful. 4.3.1 Worked Example: A Seasonal Population Function Suppose a model \(P(t) = 200 + 15t\) describes the population of a migratory bird species at a wetland, where \(t\) is measured in weeks since the birds arrive in spring, and the birds are only present at the wetland for a 20-week season. Domain: \(0 \le t \le 20\) — the model has no meaning for \(t\) outside the season, since the birds aren’t there at all. Range: at \(t=0\), \(P=200\); at \(t=20\), \(P=200+15(20)=500\). So the range is \(200 \le P \le 500\). Notice that the domain is a restriction we impose based on context (the birds are only present for part of the year), while the range is a consequence we compute from the domain and the function rule. 4.4 Graph Reading & Transformations (Introduction) Reading a graph well means being able to answer questions like: where is the function increasing or decreasing? Where is it at its highest or lowest? Does it cross the x-axis, and if so, what does that mean in context? You’ll also start noticing that many graphs are shifted, stretched, or flipped versions of a simpler graph — for example, a temperature curve shifted upward because of climate warming, or a dose-response curve stretched horizontally because a drug takes longer to act. We’ll formalize these transformations with algebra in Chapter 6; for now, practice describing them in words. Describing a Shift Two years of daily temperature data are plotted on the same axes. The second year’s curve has exactly the same shape as the first year’s, just consistently 1.5°C higher at every point in time. How would you describe this transformation in one sentence? 4.5 Multipart (Piecewise) Functions A piecewise function is defined by different formulas on different parts of its domain. These show up constantly in environmental policy — tiered water rates, emissions penalties, and staged regulatory thresholds are all piecewise by design. 4.5.1 Worked Example: A Tiered Water Rate A utility charges residential customers for water using a tiered structure: \($2.00\) per unit for the first 10 units used \($3.50\) per unit for any usage beyond 10 units If \(x\) is units used and \(C(x)\) is the total cost, this is: \[ C(x) = \begin{cases} 2.00x & 0 \le x \le 10 \\ 20.00 + 3.50(x-10) & x > 10 \end{cases} \] Notice the second piece starts from \(20.00\) — the cost of the first 10 units at the first rate — and adds the higher rate only for units beyond the 10th. Evaluating the function: How much does 15 units cost? Since \(15 > 10\), use the second piece: \[ C(15) = 20.00 + 3.50(15-10) = 20.00 + 17.50 = 37.50 \] Figure 4.1: A tiered water rate structure as a piecewise function of usage. Concept Check: Evaluating Piecewise Functions 1. Using the tiered rate function above, what is \(C(8)\)? Click to reveal answer Since \(8 \le 10\), use the first piece: \(C(8) = 2.00(8) = 16.00\). 2. Why does the second piece of the function subtract 10 from \(x\) before multiplying by 3.50? Click to reveal answer Because the higher rate only applies to units beyond the first 10 — \((x - 10)\) counts just the units in the second tier, not the full usage amount. Multiplying \(3.50\) by the full \(x\) would incorrectly charge the higher rate on the first 10 units too. 3. What input value marks the boundary between the two pieces, and what happens to the cost right at that boundary? Click to reveal answer \(x = 10\). Both pieces agree there: \(C(10) = 2.00(10) = 20.00\) from the first piece, and \(20.00 + 3.50(10-10) = 20.00\) from the second piece — the function is continuous (no jump) at the boundary, it just changes slope. 4.6 Practice: Build a Piecewise Model 4.6.1 What Are You Practicing? Building a piecewise function from a verbal description of a tiered policy or threshold-based rule, then evaluating it at specific inputs. 4.6.2 How to Approach These Problems Identify each “tier” or condition and the rule that applies within it. Write each piece using the correct rule and the correct domain restriction. Make sure the pieces agree (or intentionally disagree) at the boundary, and check that against the real-world scenario. Evaluate by first checking which piece the input falls into. 4.6.3 Practice Problems 1. A carbon tax charges nothing on a company’s first 1,000 tons of emissions per year, then \($40\) per ton for any emissions beyond that. Write \(T(x)\), the total tax owed, as a piecewise function of emissions \(x\) (in tons), and find \(T(1500)\). Click to reveal solution \[ T(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & 0 \le x \le 1000 \\ 40(x-1000) & x > 1000 \end{cases} \] \(T(1500) = 40(500) = 20{,}000\) dollars. 2. A stream is classified “impaired” if its dissolved oxygen drops below 4 mg/L. Suppose oxygen level is modeled by \(O(t) = 8 - 0.5t\) for \(t\) in days during a pollution event, valid only while \(O(t) \ge 0\). What is the domain of this model, and at what day does the stream become impaired? Click to reveal solution The model is only physically meaningful while oxygen is non-negative: \(8 - 0.5t \ge 0 \Rightarrow t \le 16\), so domain is \(0 \le t \le 16\). Impairment (\(O(t) < 4\)): \(8-0.5t < 4 \Rightarrow t > 8\), so the stream becomes impaired after day 8. 4.7 Skills Drills 4.7.1 Evaluating Functions Given \(f(x) = 3x^2 - 2x + 1\), evaluate: \(f(0)\) \(f(2)\) \(f(-1)\) \(f(a)\) (leave in terms of \(a\)) Click to reveal answers \(1\) \(3(4)-4+1 = 9\) \(3(1)+2+1 = 6\) \(3a^2-2a+1\) 4.7.2 Sketching From a Table For each table, state whether \(y\) is a function of \(x\), and explain why or why not. \(x: 1,2,3,4 \quad y: 5,7,5,9\) \(x: 1,2,2,4 \quad y: 5,7,8,9\) \(x: -1,0,1 \quad y: 2,2,2\) Click to reveal answers Yes — each \(x\) appears once, each with one \(y\). No — \(x=2\) appears twice with two different \(y\) values (7 and 8). Yes — a constant function is still a valid function; repeated \(y\) values are fine as long as each \(x\) has only one. Chapter 5 Quadratic Functions & Optimization 5.1 Learning Objectives 5.2 Quadratic Form & the Vertex 5.3 Completing the Square 5.4 Graphing Parabolas 5.5 Optimization Problems 5.6 Practice: Optimization Case Study 5.7 Skills Drills Test 2 Preview

Chapter 5 Quadratic Functions & Optimization 5.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Identify the vertex of a quadratic function and interpret it in context. Complete the square to convert between forms of a quadratic. Graph a parabola from its equation. Set up and solve optimization problems using quadratic functions. 5.2 Quadratic Form & the Vertex A quadratic function has the standard form \[ f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c \] Its graph is a parabola — it opens upward if \(a>0\) and downward if \(a<0\). The single highest or lowest point on the parabola is called the vertex, and in vertex form: \[ f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k \] the vertex is simply \((h, k)\) — you can read it directly off the equation. This is the quadratic equivalent of what point-slope form was for lines: the same function, written in a form that makes one particular feature (here, the vertex) immediately visible. For a quadratic in standard form, the vertex’s \(x\)-coordinate is: \[ x = -\frac{b}{2a} \] Why a Maximum, Not a Minimum? A parabola modeling crop yield as a function of fertilizer amount opens downward. Before doing any algebra, what does that tell you about what happens if you apply either too little or too much fertilizer? 5.3 Completing the Square Completing the square converts standard form into vertex form. The core idea: take the \(x^2\) and \(x\) terms, and add and subtract the exact amount needed to make them a perfect square trinomial. 5.3.1 Worked Example: Converting a Yield Model Suppose crop yield (in bushels/acre) as a function of fertilizer applied (\(x\), in kg/acre) is modeled by: \[ Y(x) = -2x^2 + 40x + 50 \] Step 1 — Factor \(a\) out of the first two terms. \[ Y(x) = -2(x^2 - 20x) + 50 \] Step 2 — Complete the square inside the parentheses. Take half of \(-20\), square it: \((-10)^2 = 100\). Add and subtract it inside: \[ Y(x) = -2(x^2 - 20x + 100 - 100) + 50 = -2\big[(x-10)^2 - 100\big] + 50 \] Step 3 — Distribute the \(-2\) and simplify. \[ Y(x) = -2(x-10)^2 + 200 + 50 = -2(x-10)^2 + 250 \] Step 4 — Read off the vertex and interpret. Vertex: \((10, 250)\). Since \(a = -2 < 0\), this is a maximum: applying 10 kg/acre of fertilizer is predicted to maximize yield at 250 bushels/acre. Applying more or less than 10 kg/acre — in either direction — is predicted to reduce yield. Concept Check: The Vertex Formula 1. Find the vertex of \(f(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 7\) using the vertex formula, without completing the square. Click to reveal answer \(x = -\dfrac{-12}{2(3)} = 2\). Then \(f(2) = 3(4)-12(2)+7 = 12-24+7=-5\). Vertex: \((2,-5)\). 2. A quadratic has \(a > 0\). Is its vertex a maximum or a minimum, and how do you know from the shape of the graph? Click to reveal answer Minimum. When \(a>0\), the parabola opens upward, like a bowl — the vertex sits at the bottom, the lowest point on the graph. 5.4 Graphing Parabolas Once you know the vertex and the sign of \(a\), you can sketch a parabola quickly: plot the vertex, note whether it opens up or down, and use the \(y\)-intercept (\(f(0) = c\)) as a second point to gauge the width. Figure 5.1: Crop yield as a function of fertilizer applied, with the vertex marking the yield-maximizing fertilizer rate. 5.5 Optimization Problems Many environmental questions boil down to finding a maximum or minimum of some quantity — the highest sustainable harvest, the lowest-cost containment design, the peak of a pollutant plume. When the relationship is quadratic, the vertex is the answer to the optimization question. 5.5.1 Worked Example: Minimizing the Cost of a Containment Berm An engineer needs to build a rectangular containment berm with a fixed perimeter of 200 m around a storage site, and wants to choose the dimensions that maximize the enclosed area (to contain as much runoff as possible for the same amount of berm material). Step 1 — Set up variables. Let \(x\) = one side length. Since the perimeter is fixed at 200 m, the two pairs of sides satisfy \(2x + 2y = 200\), so \(y = 100 - x\). Step 2 — Write area as a function of one variable. \[ A(x) = x \cdot y = x(100-x) = -x^2 + 100x \] Step 3 — Find the vertex. \[ x = -\frac{100}{2(-1)} = 50 \] Step 4 — Solve for the other dimension and interpret. \(y = 100 - 50 = 50\). The area-maximizing berm is a \(50\text{ m} \times 50\text{ m}\) square, enclosing \(A(50) = 50(50) = 2500\) m² — the largest possible area for that fixed amount of perimeter material. Concept Check: Interpreting Max/Min in Context 1. In the berm example, why does setting \(y = 100 - x\) let us write area as a function of \(x\) alone? Click to reveal answer Because the perimeter constraint ties \(x\) and \(y\) together — once \(x\) is chosen, \(y\) is forced to be \(100-x\) to keep the perimeter fixed at 200. Substituting removes the second variable, leaving a single-variable quadratic we can optimize with the vertex formula. 2. If the optimization problem asked to minimize cost instead of maximize area, would you still look for the vertex? Click to reveal answer Yes — the vertex is always the extreme point of a parabola, whether that extreme is a maximum or a minimum depends on the sign of \(a\), not on which type of problem you’re solving. A cost function that opens upward (\(a>0\)) has its vertex as a minimum, which is exactly what a cost-minimization problem is looking for. 5.6 Practice: Optimization Case Study 5.6.1 What Are You Practicing? Turning a verbal optimization scenario into a quadratic function of a single variable, then using the vertex to answer the original question. 5.6.2 How to Approach These Problems Identify the quantity being optimized (this becomes your output, e.g. area, cost, yield). Identify the constraint that relates your variables (a fixed perimeter, a fixed budget, a fixed total). Use the constraint to eliminate one variable, leaving a single-variable quadratic. Find the vertex and interpret both coordinates in context. 5.6.3 Practice Problems 1. A conservation group has \($6000\) to spend fencing a rectangular wildlife exclosure, where fencing costs \($30\) per meter of perimeter. They want to maximize enclosed area. Set up the area function and find the optimal dimensions. Click to reveal solution Total perimeter affordable: \(6000/30 = 200\) m, same setup as the berm example: \(2x+2y=200 \Rightarrow y=100-x\), \(A(x) = -x^2+100x\), vertex at \(x=50\), so \(y=50\) — a 50 m × 50 m square, enclosing 2500 m². 2. The height of a projected water fountain arc at a restored wetland is modeled by \(h(t) = -5t^2 + 20t\), where \(h\) is height in meters and \(t\) is time in seconds. Find the maximum height and the time at which it occurs. Click to reveal solution Vertex: \(t = -\dfrac{20}{2(-5)} = 2\). \(h(2) = -5(4)+20(2) = -20+40=20\). Maximum height of 20 m at \(t=2\) seconds. 5.7 Skills Drills 5.7.1 Completing the Square Convert each quadratic to vertex form. \(f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5\) \(f(x) = 2x^2 + 8x + 3\) \(f(x) = -x^2 + 4x\) Click to reveal answers \(f(x) = (x-3)^2 - 4\) \(f(x) = 2(x+2)^2 - 5\) \(f(x) = -(x-2)^2 + 4\) 5.7.2 Vertex-Finding Use the vertex formula to find the vertex of each function. \(f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 1\) \(f(x) = -3x^2 + 12x - 2\) \(f(x) = 0.5x^2 - 2x + 6\) Click to reveal answers \(x=2\), \(f(2) = 4-8+1=-3\): vertex \((2,-3)\) \(x=2\), \(f(2)=-12+24-2=10\): vertex \((2,10)\) \(x=2\), \(f(2)=2-4+6=4\): vertex \((2,4)\) Test 2 Preview This chapter is the first of two chapters covered on Test 2. Chapter 6 Function Composition & Inverse Functions 6.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Evaluate and interpret composite functions. Determine whether a function has an inverse, and find it when it does. Verify that two functions are inverses of each other. Apply composition and inverses to chained environmental conversions. 6.2 Composition of Functions Function composition means feeding the output of one function into another as its input. We write this as \((g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x))\) — read “\(g\) of \(f\) of \(x\),” and evaluated from the inside out: first apply \(f\), then apply \(g\) to that result. Composition shows up constantly in environmental science because measurements are often separated by several converted steps: raw sensor voltage becomes a concentration, a concentration becomes a risk score, a risk score becomes a policy decision. Each of these steps is a function, and the whole pipeline is their composition. 6.2.1 Worked Example: Depth, Pressure, and Oxygen Solubility In a lake, pressure increases with depth, and the amount of oxygen water can hold (its solubility) depends on that pressure. Suppose: \[ P(d) = 1 + 0.1d \qquad \text{(pressure in atm, as a function of depth \(d\) in meters)} \] \[ S(P) = 9 + 0.5P \qquad \text{(oxygen solubility in mg/L, as a function of pressure \(P\))} \] Step 1 — Recognize this as a composition. Solubility depends on pressure, and pressure depends on depth — so solubility as a function of depth is \(S(P(d))\), the composition \((S \circ P)(d)\). Step 2 — Substitute \(P(d)\) into \(S\). \[ S(P(d)) = 9 + 0.5\big(1+0.1d\big) = 9 + 0.5 + 0.05d = 9.5 + 0.05d \] Step 3 — Evaluate at a specific depth, say \(d = 20\) m. \[ S(P(20)) = 9.5 + 0.05(20) = 10.5 \text{ mg/L} \] Step 4 — Interpret. At 20 m depth, this model predicts an oxygen solubility of 10.5 mg/L — and we found it without separately computing pressure and then plugging it in by hand each time; the composed function \(9.5+0.05d\) directly converts depth to solubility in one step. Concept Check: Evaluating Composite Functions 1. Given \(f(x) = x+3\) and \(g(x) = 2x\), find \((g \circ f)(4)\). Click to reveal answer First \(f(4) = 7\), then \(g(7) = 14\). So \((g\circ f)(4) = 14\). 2. Using the same \(f\) and \(g\), find \((f \circ g)(4)\). Is it the same as \((g \circ f)(4)\)? Click to reveal answer First \(g(4)=8\), then \(f(8) = 11\). So \((f\circ g)(4) = 11\), which is different from \((g\circ f)(4) = 14\) — composition is generally not commutative; order matters. 6.3 Inverse Functions An inverse function, written \(f^{-1}\), “undoes” what \(f\) does: if \(f(a) = b\), then \(f^{-1}(b) = a\). Composing a function with its inverse (in either order) returns the original input: \(f^{-1}(f(x)) = x\). Not every function has an inverse. A function only has an inverse if it’s one-to-one — each output comes from exactly one input (the horizontal-line-test counterpart to the vertical-line test from Chapter 3). If a function is not one-to-one, you often can’t uniquely reverse it, because a given output could have come from more than one input. 6.3.1 Finding an Inverse Algebraically To find \(f^{-1}(x)\) from \(y = f(x)\): Swap \(x\) and \(y\). Solve the new equation for \(y\). That solved expression is \(f^{-1}(x)\). 6.3.2 Worked Example: Inverting a Dosage Model Suppose a fixed dose of a controlled substance leads to a measured blood concentration, modeled by: \[ C(x) = 2x + 1 \] where \(x\) is dose (mg) and \(C\) is resulting concentration (ng/mL). A clinician measures a concentration and wants to know what dose produced it — that requires the inverse function. Step 1 — Write as \(y = 2x+1\), then swap \(x\) and \(y\). \[ x = 2y + 1 \] Step 2 — Solve for \(y\). \[ y = \frac{x-1}{2} \] Step 3 — Write the inverse function. \[ C^{-1}(x) = \frac{x-1}{2} \] Step 4 — Use it. If a measured concentration is \(15\) ng/mL, the dose that produced it was \(C^{-1}(15) = \dfrac{15-1}{2} = 7\) mg. 6.3.3 Verifying an Inverse To check that two functions are really inverses, confirm that composing them (in either order) returns the original input. Using the example above: \[ C\big(C^{-1}(x)\big) = 2\left(\frac{x-1}{2}\right)+1 = (x-1)+1 = x \ \checkmark \] Concept Check: Checking Inverse Pairs 1. Verify that \(f(x) = 3x-6\) and \(g(x) = \dfrac{x}{3}+2\) are inverses by computing \(f(g(x))\). Click to reveal answer \[ f(g(x)) = 3\left(\frac{x}{3}+2\right)-6 = x+6-6 = x \ \checkmark \] Since composing them returns \(x\), they are inverses. 2. Does \(f(x) = x^2\) have an inverse over all real numbers? Why or why not? Click to reveal answer No — \(f(x)=x^2\) is not one-to-one over all real numbers, since, for example, both \(f(2)=4\) and \(f(-2)=4\) give the same output from two different inputs. There’s no way to uniquely “undo” it without restricting the domain (e.g., to \(x \ge 0\)). 6.4 Applications of Composition & Inverses Composition and inverses are especially powerful together: composition builds a multi-step conversion pipeline, and the inverse lets you run that pipeline backward — going from a downstream measurement back to an upstream cause. Running a Pipeline Backward Recall the depth-to-solubility composition from earlier in this chapter: \(S(P(d)) = 9.5 + 0.05d\). If a diver measures an oxygen solubility of 11.0 mg/L, how would you find the depth that produced it? You don’t need to solve it yet — just describe, in words, what algebraic step you’d take first. 6.5 Practice: Chained Environmental Conversion Model 6.5.1 What Are You Practicing? Composing multi-step environmental conversions, and inverting a composed function to work backward from a measured output to an unknown input. 6.5.2 How to Approach These Problems Identify each step of the pipeline as its own function. Compose them in the correct order — the first physical step is the innermost function. Simplify the composed function into a single expression. To invert, swap the input/output variables in the composed function and solve. 6.5.3 Practice Problems 1. A sensor’s raw voltage reading \(v\) converts to a temperature via \(T(v) = 0.5v + 10\), and that temperature affects a chemical reaction rate via \(R(T) = 2T - 5\). Find \(R(T(v))\) as a single function of \(v\), and evaluate it at \(v = 8\). Click to reveal solution \(R(T(v)) = 2(0.5v+10)-5 = v+20-5 = v+15\). At \(v=8\): \(R(T(8)) = 8+15=23\). 2. Using the depth-to-solubility model \(S(d) = 9.5+0.05d\) from earlier, find \(S^{-1}(d)\), then use it to find what depth produces a solubility of 11.0 mg/L. Click to reveal solution Swap and solve: \(d = 9.5+0.05y \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{d-9.5}{0.05}\), so \(S^{-1}(d) = \dfrac{d-9.5}{0.05}\). At \(d=11.0\): \(S^{-1}(11.0) = \dfrac{1.5}{0.05} = 30\) m. 6.6 Skills Drills 6.6.1 Finding Inverses Algebraically Find the inverse of each function. \(f(x) = x - 7\) \(f(x) = 4x\) \(f(x) = \dfrac{x}{5}+2\) \(f(x) = -3x+9\) Click to reveal answers \(f^{-1}(x) = x+7\) \(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x}{4}\) \(f^{-1}(x) = 5(x-2) = 5x-10\) \(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{9-x}{3}\) 6.6.2 Composition Practice Given \(f(x) = x+2\) and \(g(x) = x^2\), evaluate: \((f\circ g)(3)\) \((g\circ f)(3)\) \((f \circ g)(x)\), simplified \((g \circ f)(x)\), simplified Click to reveal answers \(g(3)=9\), \(f(9)=11\) \(f(3)=5\), \(g(5)=25\) \((f\circ g)(x) = x^2+2\) \((g\circ f)(x) = (x+2)^2\) Test 2 Preview This chapter concludes the material covered on Test 2 (Chapters 4–5). Chapter 7 Exponential Functions and Logarithms 7.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Model growth and decay using exponential functions. Apply the definition and properties of logarithms. Solve exponential and logarithmic equations. Shift, dilate, and reflect exponential and logarithmic graphs. 7.2 Exponential Growth & Decay An exponential function has the form \[ f(t) = a \cdot b^t \] where \(a\) is the initial value (at \(t=0\)) and \(b\) is the growth factor — the multiplier applied each time \(t\) increases by 1. If \(b>1\), the quantity grows; if \(0<b<1\), it decays. This is fundamentally different from a linear function: a linear function adds the same amount each step, while an exponential function multiplies by the same factor each step. Adding vs. Multiplying A population grows by 20 individuals every year. A different population grows by 5% every year. Both start at 400. Which one do you expect to be larger after 30 years, and why — before doing any calculation? 7.2.1 Worked Example: Population Growth An invasive plant population is measured at 400 individuals, growing by 8% per year. Step 1 — Identify \(a\) and \(b\). \(a = 400\). Growing by 8% means each year’s population is \(100\% + 8\% = 108\%\) of the previous year’s, so \(b = 1.08\). Step 2 — Write the model. \[ N(t) = 400(1.08)^t \] Step 3 — Evaluate at \(t=10\) years. \[ N(10) = 400(1.08)^{10} \approx 400(2.159) \approx 863.7 \] Step 4 — Interpret. The model predicts the population will roughly double (to about 864) after 10 years of unchecked 8% annual growth. 7.2.2 Worked Example: Radioactive Decay and Half-Life A radioactive tracer has a half-life of 8 days — meaning its activity is cut in half every 8 days, regardless of the starting amount. If the initial activity is 100 units: Step 1 — Express the decay factor per half-life period. Every 8 days, the amount is multiplied by \(\frac{1}{2}\). Step 2 — Write the model using \(t\) in days. \[ A(t) = 100\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/8} \] Notice the exponent is \(t/8\), not \(t\) — this counts how many half-lives have elapsed, so that every time \(t\) increases by 8, the exponent increases by exactly 1 (one more halving). Step 3 — Evaluate at \(t = 24\) days (three half-lives). \[ A(24) = 100\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{24/8} = 100\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 100 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 12.5 \] Step 4 — Interpret. After 24 days (three half-lives), only 12.5 of the original 100 units of activity remain. Concept Check: Growth vs. Decay 1. A pollutant concentration is modeled by \(C(t) = 50(0.85)^t\). Is this growth or decay, and by what percentage does it change each period? Click to reveal answer Decay, since \(0 < 0.85 < 1\). The concentration decreases by \(15\%\) each period (since \(1 - 0.85 = 0.15\)). 2. If a population model has growth factor \(b = 1.03\), what percentage growth rate does that represent? Click to reveal answer 3% per period, since \(b = 1 + 0.03\). 7.3 Logarithms A logarithm answers the question: “to what power must I raise the base to get this number?” Formally: \[ \log_b(x) = y \iff b^y = x \] Logarithms are the inverse of exponential functions — exactly in the sense of Chapter 5. If \(f(x) = b^x\), then \(f^{-1}(x) = \log_b(x)\). Key properties: \[ \log_b(xy) = \log_b(x) + \log_b(y) \qquad \text{(product rule)} \] \[ \log_b\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) = \log_b(x) - \log_b(y) \qquad \text{(quotient rule)} \] \[ \log_b(x^n) = n\log_b(x) \qquad \text{(power rule)} \] 7.3.1 Worked Example: The pH Scale pH is defined as \(\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]\), where \([\text{H}^+]\) is hydrogen ion concentration in moles per liter. This logarithmic definition is why pH is described on a “scale” rather than a linear measurement. A water sample has \([\text{H}^+] = 1 \times 10^{-6}\) mol/L. Step 1 — Substitute into the definition. \[ \text{pH} = -\log_{10}(1\times10^{-6}) \] Step 2 — Use the power rule. Since \(\log_{10}(10^{-6}) = -6\): \[ \text{pH} = -(-6) = 6 \] Step 3 — Interpret a one-unit change. A sample with pH 5 has \([\text{H}^+] = 10^{-5}\) mol/L — ten times the hydrogen ion concentration of the pH-6 sample above, not just “one more” unit of acidity. Every single step down the pH scale represents a tenfold increase in acidity, which is exactly what makes logarithmic scales powerful for compressing a huge range of values into a manageable number line. Concept Check: Log Properties & Graph Transformations 1. Simplify \(\log_2(8) + \log_2(4)\) using the product rule, then check by combining first. Click to reveal answer \(\log_2(8) = 3\), \(\log_2(4)=2\), sum \(=5\). Check: \(\log_2(8\cdot4) = \log_2(32) = 5\) ✓. 2. A pH 8 lake and a pH 6 stream are compared. How many times more acidic (in terms of \([\text{H}^+]\)) is the stream than the lake? Click to reveal answer The difference is 2 pH units, and each unit is a factor of 10, so the stream is \(10^2 = 100\) times more acidic than the lake. 7.4 Shifting, Dilation, and Reflection Just as with the graphs you started reading in Chapter 3, exponential and logarithmic graphs can be shifted, stretched, or flipped. For a general exponential \(f(t) = a\cdot b^{t}\), adding a constant shifts the graph vertically — this matters environmentally because it represents a baseline that a quantity decays toward (or grows from), rather than decaying all the way to zero. 7.4.1 Worked Example: Decay Toward a Nonzero Baseline A lake’s temperature after a cold snap doesn’t decay toward 0°C — it decays toward the ambient equilibrium temperature. Suppose: \[ T(t) = 5(0.9)^t + 12 \] where \(t\) is hours after the cold snap and \(T\) is temperature in °C. Figure 7.1: Lake temperature decaying toward a nonzero equilibrium baseline of 12 degrees C. As \(t\) grows large, \((0.9)^t\) shrinks toward 0, so \(T(t)\) approaches \(12\)°C — the added constant is a vertical shift that sets the baseline the decay approaches, rather than the value it decays to zero from. 7.5 Practice: Half-Life / Decay-Rate Case Study 7.5.1 What Are You Practicing? Building exponential growth/decay models from a described rate or half-life, evaluating them, and using logarithms to solve for an unknown exponent (time). 7.5.2 How to Approach These Problems Identify whether the scenario describes growth or decay, and find the growth/decay factor \(b\). Write the model \(f(t) = a\cdot b^t\) (adjusting the exponent for a stated half-life or doubling time, as needed). To solve for time given a target value, isolate the exponential term and apply a logarithm to both sides. 7.5.3 Practice Problems 1. A contaminant has a half-life of 5 years. Starting from 80 units, how much remains after 15 years? Click to reveal solution \(A(t) = 80\left(\frac12\right)^{t/5}\). At \(t=15\): \(A(15) = 80\left(\frac12\right)^3 = 80\cdot\frac18 = 10\) units. 2. A population growing as \(N(t) = 200(1.05)^t\) is predicted to reach 400 (double) at some time \(t\). Set up the equation you’d need to solve, and explain (in words) what tool from this chapter you’d need to solve it for \(t\), given that \(t\) is currently in the exponent. Click to reveal solution Equation: \(400 = 200(1.05)^t \Rightarrow 2 = (1.05)^t\). Since \(t\) is trapped in the exponent, you need to take a logarithm of both sides to bring it down: \(t = \log_{1.05}(2)\), which a calculator can evaluate (\(\approx 14.2\) years). 7.6 Skills Drills 7.6.1 Solving Exponential Equations Solve each equation for \(x\). \(2^x = 16\) \(3(2)^x = 24\) \(5^{x} = 125\) \(100(0.5)^x = 25\) Click to reveal answers \(x=4\) \((2)^x = 8 \Rightarrow x=3\) \(x=3\) \((0.5)^x = 0.25 \Rightarrow x=2\) 7.6.2 Log Rules Simplify each expression. \(\log_2(4) + \log_2(8)\) \(\log_{10}(1000) - \log_{10}(10)\) \(\log_3(9^2)\) \(\log_5(1)\) Click to reveal answers \(2+3=5\) \(3-1=2\) \(2\log_3(9) = 2(2) = 4\) \(0\) (any nonzero base raised to the 0 power is 1) Test 3 Preview This chapter is the first of two chapters covered on Test 3. Chapter 8 Rational Functions & Angular Measure 8.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Identify asymptotes and domain restrictions of a rational function. Interpret the long-run behavior of a rational function in context. Convert between degrees and radians. Compute arc length. 8.2 Rational Functions A rational function is a ratio of two polynomials, \(f(x) = \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}\). Two features matter most for interpretation: Domain restrictions: \(f(x)\) is undefined wherever \(q(x) = 0\), since division by zero is undefined. Asymptotes: a vertical asymptote occurs at an \(x\)-value where the denominator hits zero (and the numerator doesn’t); a horizontal asymptote describes the value \(f(x)\) approaches as \(x\) grows very large in either direction. 8.2.1 Worked Example: A Dilution / Equilibrium Model Suppose a pollutant is continuously discharged into a reservoir at a constant rate, while clean water also flows in and dilutes it. A model for concentration over time might be: \[ C(t) = \frac{20t + 5}{t + 2} \] where \(t\) is time in hours and \(C(t)\) is concentration in mg/L. Step 1 — Find the domain restriction. The denominator is zero when \(t = -2\). Since \(t\) represents time starting at 0, this restriction doesn’t come up in the physically meaningful part of the domain (\(t \ge 0\)) — but it’s worth noting as a place the formula itself breaks down. Step 2 — Find the horizontal asymptote. As \(t\) grows very large, both the numerator and denominator are dominated by their \(t\)-terms, so: \[ C(t) \to \frac{20t}{t} = 20 \quad \text{as } t \to \infty \] Step 3 — Interpret. No matter how long this process runs, the concentration approaches — but never quite reaches — an equilibrium value of 20 mg/L. This is a common shape for dilution problems: a rational function’s horizontal asymptote represents the long-run steady state. Figure 8.1: Pollutant concentration over time approaching a horizontal asymptote at 20 mg/L. Concept Check: Finding Asymptotes 1. For \(f(x) = \dfrac{3x+1}{x-4}\), what is the domain restriction (vertical asymptote location)? Click to reveal answer The denominator is zero at \(x=4\), so \(f\) is undefined there — a vertical asymptote at \(x=4\). 2. For \(f(x) = \dfrac{5x+2}{x+1}\), what is the horizontal asymptote, and what does it represent if \(f\) models a long-run concentration? Click to reveal answer As \(x\to\infty\), \(f(x) \to \dfrac{5x}{x} = 5\). If this models concentration over time, \(5\) is the equilibrium value the concentration approaches in the long run. 8.3 Angular Measure Angles can be measured in degrees (a full circle is \(360°\)) or radians (a full circle is \(2\pi\) radians). Radians are defined so that an angle’s measure equals the arc length it sweeps out on a unit circle (radius 1) — which is why radians, not degrees, are the natural unit for the trigonometric work in Chapters 8–10. 8.3.1 Converting Between Degrees and Radians \[ \text{radians} = \text{degrees} \times \frac{\pi}{180} \] 8.3.2 Worked Example: Converting Wind Direction A weather station reports wind direction as \(225°\) (a compass bearing). Convert this to radians. Step 1 — Apply the conversion. \[ 225 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{225\pi}{180} = \frac{5\pi}{4} \text{ radians} \] Step 2 — Sanity check. \(\frac{5\pi}{4} \approx 3.93\) radians, and since a full circle is \(2\pi \approx 6.28\) radians, this is a bit past the halfway point around the circle — consistent with \(225°\) being past \(180°\) (halfway) on a \(360°\) compass. 8.3.3 Arc Length For a circle of radius \(r\), an angle \(\theta\) measured in radians sweeps out an arc of length: \[ s = r\theta \] 8.3.4 Worked Example: Distance Along a Circular Buffer Recall the 150 m contamination buffer zone from Chapter 2, centered on the Downstream Well. Regulators want to know the length of shoreline within a \(60°\) wedge of that buffer that needs sampling. Step 1 — Convert \(60°\) to radians. \[ 60 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047 \text{ radians} \] Step 2 — Apply the arc length formula with \(r = 150\) m. \[ s = 150 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = 50\pi \approx 157.1 \text{ m} \] Step 3 — Interpret. About 157 meters of the buffer’s boundary falls within that 60° wedge and needs to be included in the sampling plan. Concept Check: Degree-Radian Conversion 1. Convert \(90°\) to radians. Click to reveal answer \(90 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\) radians. 2. A circle has radius 40 m. What arc length corresponds to an angle of \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) radians? Click to reveal answer \(s = r\theta = 40 \times \dfrac{\pi}{6} = \dfrac{40\pi}{6} = \dfrac{20\pi}{3} \approx 20.9\) m. 8.4 Practice: Dilution / Equilibrium Modeling 8.4.1 What Are You Practicing? Finding domain restrictions and horizontal asymptotes of rational functions modeling dilution or equilibrium processes, and converting between degrees, radians, and arc length for circular geometry problems. 8.4.2 How to Approach These Problems For a rational function: find where the denominator is zero (domain/vertical asymptote), then examine the ratio of leading terms as \(x\) grows large (horizontal asymptote). For angular measure: identify whether you’re starting in degrees or radians, apply the correct conversion factor, and use \(s=r\theta\) only when \(\theta\) is in radians. 8.4.3 Practice Problems 1. A treatment process models contaminant concentration as \(C(t) = \dfrac{100}{t+5}\), \(t\) in hours. Find the domain restriction and describe what happens to concentration as \(t\) grows very large. Click to reveal solution Domain restriction: \(t=-5\) (outside the physical domain \(t\ge0\)). As \(t\to\infty\), \(C(t)\to 0\) — concentration approaches zero (full dilution) in the long run, since the numerator is a constant while the denominator grows without bound. 2. A wildlife tracking collar sweeps a directional antenna through \(150°\) to scan for a signal. If the antenna’s effective range forms an arc of radius 2 km, how long is the arc swept? Click to reveal solution Convert: \(150 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}\) radians. Arc length: \(s = 2 \times \dfrac{5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \approx 5.24\) km. 8.5 Skills Drills 8.5.1 Simplifying Rational Expressions Find the domain restriction(s) of each function. \(f(x) = \dfrac{x+1}{x-3}\) \(f(x) = \dfrac{2x}{x^2-9}\) \(f(x) = \dfrac{5}{x+7}\) \(f(x) = \dfrac{x-2}{x^2-4}\) Click to reveal answers \(x=3\) \(x^2-9=0 \Rightarrow x=3, x=-3\) \(x=-7\) \(x^2-4=0 \Rightarrow x=2, x=-2\) 8.5.2 Radian Conversions Convert each angle. \(45°\) to radians \(180°\) to radians \(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\) radians to degrees \(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\) radians to degrees \(30°\) to radians Click to reveal answers \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) \(\pi\) \(60°\) \(270°\) \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\) Test 3 Preview This chapter concludes the material covered on Test 3 (Chapters 6–7). Chapter 9 Circular Motion & Trigonometry 9.1 Learning Objectives 9.2 Circular Motion Basics 9.3 Right-Triangle Trigonometry 9.4 The Unit Circle 9.5 Core Trig Identities (Introduction) 9.6 Practice: Modeling a Rotational/Cyclical Process 9.7 Skills Drills Test 4 Preview

Chapter 9 Circular Motion & Trigonometry 9.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Describe circular motion using angle and radius. Set up and solve right-triangle trigonometry problems. Evaluate trigonometric functions using the unit circle. Recognize core trigonometric identities. 9.2 Circular Motion Basics Many environmental processes are cyclical — they repeat in a regular pattern over time, tracing out something conceptually similar to a point moving around a circle. Seasonal daylight hours, tidal height, and the rotation of a wind turbine blade all share this underlying structure: a quantity that sweeps through an angle at a steady rate and returns to where it started. If an object moves around a circle of radius \(r\) at a constant angular speed \(\omega\) (radians per unit time), the angle swept out after time \(t\) is: \[ \theta(t) = \omega t \] and, from Chapter 7, the arc length traveled is \(s = r\theta = r\omega t\). Two Kinds of Speed A large wind turbine blade and a small one rotate at the same angular speed (say, one full rotation every 4 seconds). Which blade’s tip is moving faster through the air — the long one or the short one? Why, even though their angular speed is identical? 9.2.1 Worked Example: A Turbine Blade Tip Speed A wind turbine blade is 40 m long and completes one full rotation every 4 seconds. Step 1 — Find the angular speed. One full rotation is \(2\pi\) radians, completed in 4 seconds: \[ \omega = \frac{2\pi \text{ radians}}{4 \text{ s}} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ radians/s} \] Step 2 — Find the linear (tip) speed using \(s = r\theta\), applied as a rate. Since \(s = r\omega t\), the tip’s speed is \(r\omega\): \[ v = r\omega = 40 \times \frac{\pi}{2} = 20\pi \approx 62.8 \text{ m/s} \] Step 3 — Interpret. The blade tip moves at roughly 63 m/s — over 225 km/h — even though the whole blade completes the same angular rotation as any point closer to the hub. Points farther from the center cover more linear distance in the same rotation, which is exactly the intuition from the prompt box above. 9.3 Right-Triangle Trigonometry For a right triangle with one acute angle \(\theta\), the three basic trigonometric ratios relate the angle to the triangle’s side lengths: \[ \sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \qquad \cos(\theta) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \qquad \tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} \] 9.3.1 Worked Example: Estimating Canopy Height An ecologist stands 30 m from the base of a tree and measures the angle of elevation to its top as \(35°\). Step 1 — Identify the triangle. The horizontal distance (30 m) is the side adjacent to the angle; the tree height is the side opposite the angle. Step 2 — Choose the ratio that relates opposite and adjacent. \[ \tan(35°) = \frac{\text{height}}{30} \] Step 3 — Solve for height. \[ \text{height} = 30\tan(35°) \approx 30(0.7002) \approx 21.0 \text{ m} \] Step 4 — Interpret. The tree is approximately 21 meters tall — measured without ever needing to climb it or use a direct measuring tool, just an angle and a horizontal distance. Concept Check: Right-Triangle Trig Setup 1. In the canopy example, why was tangent the right ratio to use, rather than sine or cosine? Click to reveal answer Tangent relates the opposite and adjacent sides, and those are exactly the two sides we knew or wanted (adjacent = 30 m horizontal distance, opposite = the unknown height). Sine and cosine both involve the hypotenuse, which we didn’t know here. 2. A ramp rises 2 m over a horizontal run of 15 m. What angle does the ramp make with the ground? Click to reveal answer \(\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{2}{15} \Rightarrow \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{15}\right) \approx 7.6°\). 9.4 The Unit Circle The unit circle is a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin. For any angle \(\theta\) (measured counterclockwise from the positive x-axis), the point where the angle’s ray crosses the unit circle has coordinates \((\cos\theta, \sin\theta)\). This is what lets sine and cosine be defined for any angle, not just acute angles inside a right triangle. Key standard angles (in radians, with degrees noted): \(\theta\) \(0\) \(\pi/6\) (30°) \(\pi/4\) (45°) \(\pi/3\) (60°) \(\pi/2\) (90°) \(\cos\theta\) \(1\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\) \(\dfrac12\) \(0\) \(\sin\theta\) \(0\) \(\dfrac12\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\) \(1\) 9.5 Core Trig Identities (Introduction) The single most important trig identity comes directly from the unit circle being, well, a circle of radius 1 — so every point \((\cos\theta, \sin\theta)\) on it satisfies the circle equation from Chapter 2 with \(r=1\): \[ \sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1 \] This is the Pythagorean identity, and it holds for every angle \(\theta\), not just the standard ones in the table above. Concept Check: Unit Circle Values 1. Using the unit circle, what is \(\cos(\pi/3)\)? Click to reveal answer \(\cos(\pi/3) = \dfrac12\). 2. If \(\sin(\theta) = 0.6\), use the Pythagorean identity to find \(\cos(\theta)\), assuming \(\theta\) is in the first quadrant (so cosine is positive). Click to reveal answer \(\cos^2(\theta) = 1 - 0.6^2 = 1-0.36=0.64 \Rightarrow \cos(\theta) = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8\). 9.6 Practice: Modeling a Rotational/Cyclical Process 9.6.1 What Are You Practicing? Connecting angular speed to linear (tip) speed, solving right-triangle problems for an unknown side or angle, and applying the Pythagorean identity. 9.6.2 How to Approach These Problems For rotational speed problems: find angular speed first (angle swept ÷ time), then multiply by radius for linear speed. For right-triangle problems: label which sides are opposite/adjacent relative to the angle you’re using, then choose the matching ratio. For identity problems: substitute the known value into \(\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\) and solve for the unknown, keeping track of sign based on which quadrant the angle is in. 9.6.3 Practice Problems 1. A buoy bobs in a circular tidal eddy of radius 5 m, completing one loop every 20 seconds. Find its angular speed and its linear speed. Click to reveal solution \(\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{20} = \dfrac{\pi}{10}\) rad/s. Linear speed: \(v = r\omega = 5\times\dfrac{\pi}{10} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.57\) m/s. 2. A drone hovers and measures the angle of depression to a wildlife carcass on the ground as \(50°\), from a horizontal distance of 12 m. How high is the drone? Click to reveal solution \(\tan(50°) = \dfrac{\text{height}}{12} \Rightarrow \text{height} = 12\tan(50°) \approx 12(1.19) \approx 14.3\) m. 9.7 Skills Drills 9.7.1 Evaluating Trig Functions at Standard Angles Evaluate each without a calculator, using the unit circle table above. \(\sin(\pi/6)\) \(\cos(\pi/4)\) \(\tan(\pi/3)\) \(\sin(\pi/2)\) \(\cos(0)\) Click to reveal answers \(\dfrac12\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\) \(\dfrac{\sin(\pi/3)}{\cos(\pi/3)} = \dfrac{\sqrt3/2}{1/2} = \sqrt3\) \(1\) \(1\) Test 4 Preview This chapter is the first of three chapters covered on Test 4. Chapter 10 Trigonometric Functions 10.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Graph the six trigonometric functions. Identify amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift of a sinusoidal function. Evaluate inverse trigonometric functions. 10.2 Graphs of Trig Functions The graphs of \(\sin(\theta)\) and \(\cos(\theta)\) are smooth, repeating waves — direct pictures of the unit-circle coordinates from Chapter 8, plotted as \(\theta\) sweeps from \(0\) to \(2\pi\) and beyond. Both have the same basic wave shape; \(\cos(\theta)\) is simply \(\sin(\theta)\) shifted left by \(\pi/2\). Figure 10.1: Graphs of sine and cosine over two full periods. Every point where the curve crosses zero, reaches its peak, or reaches its trough tells you something about the cycle — and that vocabulary (amplitude, period, phase shift, vertical shift) is exactly what lets us fit these curves to real cyclical data. 10.3 Sinusoidal Functions A general sinusoidal function is written: \[ f(t) = A\sin\big(B(t-C)\big) + D \] Amplitude (\(A\)): half the distance between the peak and trough — how far the curve swings above and below its midline. Period (\(2\pi/B\)): how long one complete cycle takes. Phase shift (\(C\)): how far the curve is shifted horizontally. Vertical shift (\(D\)): the midline the curve oscillates around. 10.3.1 Worked Example: Modeling Daylight Hours Suppose daylight hours in Seattle over the course of a year are approximately: \[ H(t) = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 80)\right) + 12.2 \] where \(t\) is day of the year (\(t=1\) is Jan 1) and \(H(t)\) is hours of daylight. Step 1 — Read off each parameter. \(A = 3.5\), \(B = \dfrac{2\pi}{365}\), \(C = 80\), \(D = 12.2\). Step 2 — Interpret each one. Amplitude \(3.5\): daylight swings 3.5 hours above and below the midline. Period \(= \dfrac{2\pi}{2\pi/365} = 365\) days — one full cycle per year, as expected. Phase shift \(80\): the curve is shifted so that day 80 (roughly the spring equinox) is where daylight is at the midline and increasing — a reasonable match for when day length crosses its average value on the way up. Vertical shift \(12.2\): the average day length across the year is 12.2 hours. Step 3 — Predict the longest day. The maximum of \(\sin\) is 1, so the longest day has \(H = 3.5(1)+12.2 = 15.7\) hours. Figure 10.2: Modeled daylight hours across a calendar year. Concept Check: Identifying Amplitude/Period/Shift 1. For \(f(t) = 4\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) + 10\), identify the amplitude, period, and vertical shift. Click to reveal answer Amplitude \(=4\). Period \(= \dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6} = 12\). Vertical shift \(=10\) (no phase shift, since there’s no \((t-C)\) term). 2. If a tidal height model has amplitude 1.2 m and vertical shift 3 m, what are the highest and lowest tide heights it predicts? Click to reveal answer Highest: \(3+1.2 = 4.2\) m. Lowest: \(3-1.2 = 1.8\) m. 10.4 Inverse Trigonometric Functions Just as with the general inverses from Chapter 5, an inverse trig function undoes a trig function — but with a caveat: since sine, cosine, and tangent are not one-to-one (they repeat forever), their inverses are only defined by restricting to one cycle’s worth of output. \(\sin^{-1}(x)\) (also written \(\arcsin(x)\)) returns an angle between \(-\pi/2\) and \(\pi/2\); \(\cos^{-1}(x)\) returns an angle between \(0\) and \(\pi\). 10.4.1 Worked Example: Solving for Time-of-Year Using the daylight model \(H(t) = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right)+12.2\), find a day of the year when daylight is exactly 14 hours. Step 1 — Isolate the sine term. \[ 14 - 12.2 = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right) \Rightarrow \sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right) = \frac{1.8}{3.5} \approx 0.514 \] Step 2 — Apply the inverse sine. \[ \frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80) = \sin^{-1}(0.514) \approx 0.539 \text{ radians} \] Step 3 — Solve for \(t\). \[ t - 80 = 0.539 \times \frac{365}{2\pi} \approx 31.3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \approx 111.3 \] Step 4 — Interpret. Day 111 of the year (roughly late April) is predicted to have 14 hours of daylight. (Because sine repeats, there’s a second day later in the year with the same daylight total, on the way back down — inverse sine only hands you one of the two solutions directly.) 10.5 Practice: Fit a Sinusoidal Model to Seasonal Data 10.5.1 What Are You Practicing? Reading amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift off a sinusoidal equation or graph, and using inverse trig to solve for an unknown input. 10.5.2 How to Approach These Problems Match the given equation to the general form \(A\sin(B(t-C))+D\) term by term. Compute period as \(2\pi/B\) if you need it. To solve for an unknown \(t\), isolate the sine or cosine term first, then apply the inverse function, then finish solving algebraically. 10.5.3 Practice Problems 1. Tidal height at a harbor is modeled by \(y(t) = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) + 2.5\), \(t\) in hours after midnight. Find the amplitude, period, and predicted high and low tide heights. Click to reveal solution Amplitude \(=1.5\). Period \(=\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6}=12\) hours. High tide: \(2.5+1.5=4.0\) m. Low tide: \(2.5-1.5=1.0\) m. 2. Using the same tidal model, find a time \(t\) (between 0 and 6) when the tide height is exactly 3.5 m. Click to reveal solution \(3.5-2.5 = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) = \dfrac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.667\). Then \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t = \sin^{-1}(0.667)\approx 0.730\), so \(t \approx 0.730\times\dfrac{6}{\pi}\approx 1.39\) hours after midnight. 10.6 Skills Drills 10.6.1 Graphing Transformed Sinusoids For each function, state the amplitude, period, and vertical shift. \(f(t) = 2\sin(t) + 5\) \(f(t) = 6\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}t\right)\) \(f(t) = 0.5\sin(2t) - 3\) \(f(t) = 10\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}t\right) + 20\) Click to reveal answers Amplitude 2, period \(2\pi\), vertical shift 5 Amplitude 6, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/4}=8\), vertical shift 0 Amplitude 0.5, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{2}=\pi\), vertical shift \(-3\) Amplitude 10, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/12}=24\), vertical shift 20 10.6.2 Evaluating Inverse Trig Functions Evaluate each (in radians). \(\sin^{-1}(1)\) \(\cos^{-1}(0)\) \(\sin^{-1}(0.5)\) \(\cos^{-1}(1)\) Click to reveal answers \(\pi/2\) \(\pi/2\) \(\pi/6\) \(0\) Test 4 Preview This chapter is the second of three chapters covered on Test 4.
