Chapter 11 Sinusoidal Equations & Modeling 11.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Solve sinusoidal equations for specific input values. Carry out the full modeling workflow: data -> equation -> prediction. Synthesize precalculus tools across the course in a capstone project. 11.2 Solving Sinusoidal Equations Solving a sinusoidal equation for a specific input follows the same pattern as the inverse-trig worked example in Chapter 9: isolate the sine (or cosine) term, apply the inverse function, then finish the algebra. The one new wrinkle worth naming explicitly here: because sine and cosine repeat every cycle, a sinusoidal equation almost always has more than one valid solution within a given time span, even though the inverse function only ever returns one of them directly. 11.2.1 Worked Example: Two Solutions, One Cycle Using the harbor tide model from Chapter 9, \(y(t) = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right)+2.5\), find all times \(t\) between 0 and 12 hours when the tide is at 3.5 m. Step 1 — Isolate and apply the inverse, as before. \[ \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) = \frac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.667 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\pi}{6}t = \sin^{-1}(0.667) \approx 0.730 \] \[ t \approx 0.730 \times \frac{6}{\pi} \approx 1.39 \text{ hours} \] This matches the single solution found in Chapter 9 — but it isn’t the only time in the 12-hour window when the tide hits 3.5 m. Step 2 — Find the second solution using symmetry. Sine is symmetric about its peak: if \(\theta_0\) is one solution to \(\sin(\theta) = k\), then \(\pi - \theta_0\) is the other solution within one cycle. So: \[ \frac{\pi}{6}t = \pi - 0.730 \approx 2.412 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t \approx 2.412\times\frac{6}{\pi}\approx 4.61 \text{ hours} \] Step 3 — Interpret. The tide reaches 3.5 m twice in this 12-hour cycle: once around \(t\approx1.39\) hours (rising toward high tide) and again around \(t\approx4.61\) hours (falling back down from high tide). Reporting only the first answer from the inverse sine would have missed half the picture. Why Two Solutions? Sketch (or picture) the tide curve from the example above. Mark the horizontal line \(y=3.5\). How many times does the curve cross that line between \(t=0\) and \(t=12\)? Does that match the two solutions found above? Concept Check: Solving for a Specific Time 1. For \(y(t) = 4\sin(t)\), one solution to \(y(t)=2\) is \(t = \sin^{-1}(0.5) = \pi/6\). What is the second solution within \(0\) to \(2\pi\)? Click to reveal answer Using the symmetry rule: \(t = \pi - \pi/6 = 5\pi/6\). 11.3 The Full Modeling Workflow Every chapter in this book has built a piece of the same workflow: take a real quantity, decide what family of function best matches how it behaves, fit that function’s parameters to data, and use the resulting equation to predict, interpret, or solve. This section walks through that workflow start to finish, on a single dataset. 11.3.1 Worked Example: From Data to Prediction Suppose a research buoy records water temperature at the surface of a lake every month for a year, and the values trace out a clear seasonal cycle: a summer peak around 22°C and a winter low around 6°C, with the peak occurring near the start of August (month 8, if January is month 1). Step 1 — Choose the function family. A repeating annual cycle is a strong signal for a sinusoidal function (Chapter 9), not linear, exponential, or rational — this is the “shape of change” recognition skill from the very first chapter of this book, now applied to real data. Step 2 — Extract amplitude and vertical shift from the peak and trough. \[ D = \frac{22+6}{2} = 14, \qquad A = \frac{22-6}{2} = 8 \] Step 3 — Set the period. One full annual cycle: period \(=12\) months, so \(B = \dfrac{2\pi}{12} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\). Step 4 — Set the phase shift using the peak location. Sine reaches its maximum a quarter-cycle (3 months) after it crosses the midline going up. Since the peak is at month 8, the midline-crossing (going up) is at month 5, so \(C = 5\). Step 5 — Assemble the model. \[ T(t) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-5)\right) + 14 \] Step 6 — Use it to predict. What temperature does the model predict for month 11 (November)? \[ T(11) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(11-5)\right)+14 = 8\sin(\pi)+14 = 8(0)+14 = 14°C \] Figure 11.1: A fitted sinusoidal model of lake surface temperature across a year, with the November prediction marked. 11.4 Capstone Case Study Putting It All Together A watershed manager wants to predict the seasonal peak concentration of a nutrient that spikes each spring due to snowmelt runoff, then decays over the summer as flows subside. Using tools from across this book, sketch — in words — how you would approach this: What shape of function would you use for the spring spike and rise? (Chapter 4 or 9?) What shape of function would you use for the summer decay? (Chapter 6?) What would you need to know about the data to fit the parameters of your chosen function(s)? 11.5 Practice: Capstone Modeling Project 11.5.1 What You’re Practicing Choosing the right function family from the whole course based on a system’s described behavior, fitting parameters from given features (peak, trough, timing, rate), and using the resulting model to predict or solve. 11.5.2 Practice Problems 1. A river’s turbidity (cloudiness) rises sharply after each rainstorm and then decays back toward a clear baseline over the following days, in a pattern that repeats after every storm but doesn’t cycle on a fixed calendar schedule. Which function family from this book best matches a single post-storm decay — exponential, sinusoidal, or quadratic? Explain your reasoning in one sentence. Click to reveal solution Exponential. A single storm’s turbidity spike decaying back toward a baseline is a decay-toward-equilibrium pattern (like the lake temperature after a cold snap in Chapter 6) — it isn’t a repeating annual cycle (which would call for sinusoidal), and it isn’t described by a single symmetric peak the way a quadratic optimization problem is. 2. A coastal air temperature cycles annually with a high of 26°C in July (month 7) and a low of 10°C in January (month 1). Build a sinusoidal model \(T(t)\) for this cycle, and predict the temperature in April (month 4). Click to reveal solution \(D = \dfrac{26+10}{2}=18\), \(A=\dfrac{26-10}{2}=8\), period 12 months so \(B=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\). Peak at month 7 means midline-crossing (rising) at month 4, so \(C=4\): \[ T(t) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-4)\right)+18 \] At \(t=4\): \(T(4) = 8\sin(0)+18 = 18°C\) — which makes sense, since month 4 was defined as the midline-crossing point itself. 11.6 Skills Drills: Mixed Review A short mixed set, spanning tools from across the whole course. Precalculus Equation Sheet

Precalculus Equation Sheet Lines & Circles Concept Form Slope-intercept \(y = mx + b\) Point-slope \(y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)\) Circle \((x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2\) Distance formula \(d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}\) Quadratics Concept Form Standard form \(f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c\) Vertex form \(f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k\) Vertex (from standard form) \(x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}\) Exponential & Logarithmic Concept Form Exponential growth/decay \(f(t) = a \cdot b^t\) Logarithm definition \(\log_b(x) = y \iff b^y = x\) Product rule \(\log_b(xy) = \log_b(x) + \log_b(y)\) Quotient rule \(\log_b(x/y) = \log_b(x) - \log_b(y)\) Power rule \(\log_b(x^n) = n\log_b(x)\) Rational Functions & Angular Measure Concept Form Degrees to radians \(\text{radians} = \text{degrees} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}\) Arc length \(s = r\theta\) Trigonometry & Sinusoidal Functions Concept Form Sinusoidal function \(f(t) = A\sin\big(B(t-C)\big) + D\) Amplitude \(A\) Period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{B}\) Phase shift \(C\) Vertical shift \(D\) Pythagorean identity \(\sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1\)
